title: Quick check: the ccTLD four-dimensional pattern

questions.prompt: A UK-based client (no Australian presence) wants `excellentbiz.com.au` to market services in Australia. They have no ABN, no ACN, and no Australian agent. What's the calibrated response?
choices: Register it; .com.au eligibility is loose for foreign clients.

Surface the constraint: .com.au requires an Australian commercial entity with ABN or ACN. Foreign-only entities don't qualify. The client's options are (a) form an Australian subsidiary or branch, (b) use a local agent that can hold the registration, or (c) pick a different TLD (.com, .biz, .au's open second-level via auDA's policy).
Refuse and tell the client to drop the Australian expansion.
Submit anyway and let auDA sort it out.
questions.explanation: The eligibility + presence dimensions both apply to `.com.au`. A foreign entity without Australian presence doesn't qualify. The constructive path is naming the gap and offering real options; submitting blind wastes the client's payment, and refusing on principle (when alternatives exist) over-rotates. The .au second-level is more open under recent auDA policy but still requires Australian presence.

questions.prompt: A client with an Australian sole-trader ABN successfully registered `freshfoods.com.au` last week. They now ask for `freshfoods.org.au` 'for the social impact side of the business.' Does the same ABN qualify?
choices: Yes, if the ABN qualifies for `.com.au`, it qualifies for `.org.au`.
No. `.org.au` is a restricted second-level name with its own eligibility (non-profits and charities); a sole-trader commercial ABN typically doesn't qualify.
Yes, `.org` is open globally so `.org.au` is also open.
Refuse to register any ccTLD second-level name other than `.com.<cc>`.
questions.explanation: Same registry, different restricted-second-level-name rules, different outcome. The global `.org` is open, but `.org.au` is a separate namespace under auDA with its own restrictions. Surface the constraint to the client and offer alternatives (`.net.au` is open to commercial entities).

questions.prompt: A registrar's panel shows `.uk` as available for a name the client wants. The registrar's marketing summary says 'no eligibility restrictions.' Should you trust the summary?
choices: Yes, the registrar's panel is authoritative.
Yes for `.uk` specifically because Nominet is well-known.
Verify by checking Nominet's own published policy before quoting the client.
Submit the registration; the registry will refuse it if there's an issue.
questions.explanation: Registry policy pages are the source of truth; registrar summaries are convenience. The discipline is consistent regardless of the TLD's reputation. Nominet does run an open `.uk` registry, but the practice of verifying via the registry's own page applies to every ccTLD.
